coredata.dc:description: Rapid global industrialization and an increase in population have enhanced the risk of heavy metals accumulation in plant bodies to disrupt the morphological, biochemical, and physiological processes of plants. To cope with this situation, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) NPs were used first time to mitigate abiotic stresses caused in plant. In this study, rGO NPs were synthesized and reduced with Tecoma stans plant leave extract through modified Hummer's methods. The well prepared rGO NPs were characterized by ultra-violet visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Zeta potential, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). However, pot experiment was conducted with four different concentrations (15, 30, 60, 120 mg/L) of rGO NPs and three different concentrations (300, 500,700 mg/L) of lead (Pb) stress were applied. To observe the mitigative effects of rGO NPs, 30 mg/L of rGO NPs and 700 mg/L of Pb were used in combination. Changes in morphological and biochemical characteristics of wheat plants were observed for both Pb stress and rGO NPs treatments. Pb was found to inhibit the morphological and biochemical characteristics of plants. rGO NPs alone as well as in combination with Pb was found to increase the chlorophyll content of wheat plants. Under Pb stress conditions and rGO NPs treatments, antioxidant enzyme activities like ascorbate peroxidases (APX), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) were observed. Current findings revealed that greenly reduced graphene oxide NPs can effectively promote growth in wheat plants under Pb stress by elevating chlorophyll content of leaves, reducing the Pb uptake, and suppressing ROS produced due to Pb toxicity.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid global industrialization and an increase in population have enhanced the risk of heavy metals accumulation in plant bodies to disrupt the morphological, biochemical, and physiological processes of plants. To cope with this situation, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) NPs were used first time to mitigate abiotic stresses caused in plant. In this study, rGO NPs were synthesized and reduced with Tecoma stans plant leave extract through modified Hummer's methods. The well prepared rGO NPs were characterized by ultra-violet visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Zeta potential, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). However, pot experiment was conducted with four different concentrations (15, 30, 60, 120 mg/L) of rGO NPs and three different concentrations (300, 500,700 mg/L) of lead (Pb) stress were applied. To observe the mitigative effects of rGO NPs, 30 mg/L of rGO NPs and 700 mg/L of Pb were used in combination. Changes in morphological and biochemical characteristics of wheat plants were observed for both Pb stress and rGO NPs treatments. Pb was found to inhibit the morphological and biochemical characteristics of plants. rGO NPs alone as well as in combination with Pb was found to increase the chlorophyll content of wheat plants. Under Pb stress conditions and rGO NPs treatments, antioxidant enzyme activities like ascorbate peroxidases (APX), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) were observed. Current findings revealed that greenly reduced graphene oxide NPs can effectively promote growth in wheat plants under Pb stress by elevating chlorophyll content of leaves, reducing the Pb uptake, and suppressing ROS produced due to Pb toxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals (HMs) as a soil constituent naturally exist in the Earth's crust (Aslam et al., 2021). Due to the stability and non-degradability of heavy metals, they persistently accumulate in the environment for a long time (Ghnaya et al., 2013). Non-essential heavy metals (HMs) i.e., Pb, cadmium, mercury, and silver enter the cell and producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Zulfiqar et al., 2019) which altered the cellular functions (Arshad and Mustafa, 2023). Among the hazardous HMs, Pb is the second most hazardous due to its non-degradability and stable nature in the environment US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has listed it as a human carcinogen (Esposito et al., 2023). Agricultural soils are being contaminated with Pb mainly due to anthropogenic activities and is a widespread problem in agroforest land (Luo et al., 2024). An increase in exogenous Pb levels increases the accumulation of Pb in plant tissues. Several physiological and biochemical changes are being caused by Pb and can equally affect seed germination, plant growth, water status of the soil, and nitrate assimilation (Souahi et al., 2021). In the past three centuries, environmental Pb concentration has increased up to 1000-fold mainly due to large industrial demands and almost no recycling or less recycling of the Pb to reduce it, which results in an accumulation in the plants and animals' bodies (Dias et al., 2019; Kershner et al., 2022; Shehzad et al., 2023).

Pb Contamination of soil depends on; the concentration of Pb pollutants in soil, inhibition of plant growth, decrease in chlorophyll and photosynthetic activity, and change in anti-enzymatic activities and accumulation of acid-reactive substances of thiobarbituric and H2O2 excessively in plant tissues (Pourrut et al., 2011). Pb enters like other HMs into the plant cells and induces oxidative stress in plants due to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the growing parts of the plant which in turn results in a reduction of productivity of the plant (Navabpour et al., 2020; Wani et al., 2015). Antioxidant systems of the plant protect plants from corrosive and degraded effects caused by any means (Mustafa et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2023). All these complex detoxifications are highly compartmentalized processes in plant cells (Zaheer et al., 2023). Recently, there were many new methods and techniques have been introduced to mitigate or combat exogenous stresses but the application of nanomaterials is very competitive, less expensive, easy to handle with extraordinary properties, and wide range of applications all over the globe (Hasan et al., 2013; Mustafa et al., 2021). Utilization and applications of different types of biocompatible nanomaterials have improved the growth performance, Tissue mineralization, antioxidant capacity, and immune response of domesticated living (Luo et al., 2021), phytotoxicity evolution in agroforestry (Hasan et al., 2021), and diagnosis of many lethal diseases at a very early stage in the biomedical field (Cao et al., 2020).

Graphene oxide (GO) is a new emerging nanomaterial with unique and small sizes, multiple shapes, and geometry. However, its distinctive physiochemical properties and novel surface area make it widely used in the fields of medicine, electronics, energy, cosmetics and agricultural products all over the world (Yadav et al., 2022). Right now, there are many industries producing hundreds of daily basis supplements with graphene-based materials or products globally, some of the well-known ones are IBM and Intel (Jiříčková et al., 2022; Park et al., 2020). By the end of 2020, the market values for GO based products will be projected to touch the value of 675 million US dollars with approximation and is expected to be more in the coming years due to its high demand, which is expected to cause a considerable amount of graphene-based waste addition and accumulation in the ecosystem (Park et al., 2020).

Some of the researchers have found significant effects of GO on the growth and development of plants (Ghorbanpour et al., 2018; Hatami et al., 2019; Ahmadi et al., 2024). These effects are variable and can be influenced by certain factors i.e., concentration of GO and genotype (Al-Rawashdeh et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021). It increases mineral micronutrient uptake of plants as a carrier in soil. It positively affects soil nutritional traits and has a positive impact on plant growth, physiology and benefits by protecting against other phytotoxic agents (Hammerschmiedt et al., 2023). However, the impact of GO on plants is dose-dependent. From some of the experimental work, it has been shown that functional GO in certain plants, at low concentrations, can stimulate physiological processes. Germination of aged seeds (Hasan et al., 2022a; Javed et al., 2023) and root differentiation of wheat plants could be promoted by treating them with 200 mg/L of hydrated graphene ribbon (Lopes et al., 2021).

Wheat is one of staple foods and is biologically known as Triticum aestivum L. It is the second most cultivated crop after rice in world. The grain of wheat is used in the diet and shows a significant role in the nutritional quality of food for human health. Triticum aestivum L is an important food crop around the world due to its nutritional value in composition form (Jayara et al., 2023). Around 36% of the world population depends on wheat to meet their food need due to which is badged as a staple food (Mottaleb et al., 2023). However, decreas in productivity have been found due to certain biotic and abiotic stress conditions in the environment (Poole et al., 2021). Wheat production has declined in the last few years due to various environmental conditions i.e., salinity, water logging, drought, and proper lack of fertilizers. Moreover, certain other biotic and abiotic stresses also reduced wheat production to a considerable level (Arshad and Mustafa, 2023; Mustafa and Komatsu, 2023).

Current year, the production of wheat has significantly reduced to exogenous as well as internal factors as compared to previous years. Pb one of the toxic heavy metals released into the ecosystem negatively affects plant growth and productivity. In addition, Pb as a toxicant and stress inducer, disturb the normal physiological functions of plants such as photosynthesis, homeostasis,the and other regulatory process. Furthermore, this intoxication of Pb also continues in environmental food chain which in turn causes serious issues for animals and humans health.

In this study, GO was prepared and then biologically reduced through the green chemistry method by using Tecoma stans plant leaf extract. Tecoma stans, also show antioxidant, wound healing, antispasmodic, antiproliferative, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, antifungal, and cytotoxic properties. All these medical importance because of very active biocomponents such as flavonoids, protein, carbohydrates, monoterpene alkaloids, and indoles (such as tryptophan, tryptamine, and skatole), in addition to anthranilic acid (Gonçalves et al., 2022; Hafsi et al., 2022). This study aimed to investigate the mitigation of Pb stress by rGO NPs in wheat plants by observing morphological changes such as shoot length, root length, and fresh weight of the plant. Moreover, under Pb stress, antioxidant enzyme activities such as SOD, POD, APX, and CAT were investigated in wheat plants.

A pot experiment was performed to evaluate the Triticum aestivum and its susceptibility index under heavy metals stress and NPs. The details of materials and methods used for experimentation and its evaluation are mentioned below.

1 g of graphite was added to 17.5 mL of H2SO4 which was stirred for 45 min. Afterward, 0.5 g of NaNO3 was added and the solution was further stirred for 15 min. Then 3 g of KMnO4 were slowly added by keeping the temperature below 10 °C and continued stirring for 10–15 min. After that, the solution was placed on a hot plate with continuous stirring for 1 h at 40 °C. Distilled water was added to this mixture dropwise by keeping the temperature below 10 °C. The color of the mixture turned brown which indicated the formation of GO.

Tecoma stans leaves were collected from Quaid I Azam University Islamabad. The leaves were properly washed to remove the dust and other contaminations. In this study, method was followed and modified by previous research (Hasan et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2023a; Saif et al., 2021). The leaves were weighed 40 g, cut down into small pieces, and then ground with mortar and pestle by gradually adding 150 mL of water. The solution was heated on the hot plate until its color changed to yellowish. The plant extract was then filtered with Whatman No 42- filter paper. The plant extract was added to the mixture stirred and heated at 70 °C for 10 h. To evaporate the solvent and leave the reducing and capping agent behind. The color of the mixture turned black. 75 mL of H2O2 was added drop by drop to stop further reaction. The color of the mixture was turned into black by adding 75 mL of H2O2 drop by drop till the end of reaction. Later, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, supernatant was discorded, and the pellet was washed three times with 3% HCl and then with distilled water twice. The blackish brown paste obtained was then kept in the oven for 24 h at 50 °C and blackish flakes.

For the determination of UV of rGO, samples were collected at different intervals during preparation, and absorbance was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 200–600 nm wavelengths. The presence of different functional groups in rGO NPs was identified with the help of infrared spectra in transmission mode with the help of a Fourier-transformed Infrared Spectrophotometer (FT/IR-610, JASCO) keeping wavelength in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu Model: XRD 6000) was used for the XRD investigation of rGO NPs in the powder form in the range of 20–80°at 2 theta which is equal to 0.154 nm in wavelength. The zeta potential values were measured through Zetasizer Ver.8.00.4813 for rGO NPs in solution form. The morphology, texture, and surface analysis of rGO NPs powder form were studied using a Quanta Inspect scanning electron microscope, operating at 25 kV in vacuum (SEM: JEOL JSM-5800 LV/EDS). For EDX analysis of rGO NPs powder air-dried samples were recorded to determine the crystal structure and size of the nanoparticles using XRD spectroscopy (Rigaku, Ultima IV, X-ray diffractometer system). Using Cu Kα ration voltage, all diffracted intensities were recorded at 40 KV and 30 mA current in the 20−80 scanning range.

The experimental works were carried out at the Plant Sciences Department, Quaid-I-Azam University, Islamabad. The experimental procedures were carried out at the Plant Proteomics Laboratory, Quaid-I-Azam University, Islamabad. A complete setup was designed to carry out the experimental work in a pre-planned manner. According to the need for and importance of each experimental analysis, three replicates were used to get the mean values for all the parameters. The detailed work for each single parameter of all the replicates was kept on record for further analyses. Triticum aestivum (Wheat plant), variety Akbar et al., 2018, was selected for the experimental work. The sand was selected as the growth medium, which was properly bleached for 24 h to remove all the impurities and then washed with tap water 15 times to remove the bleach and the mineral nutrients up to the maximum. Seeds of Triticum aestivum were obtained from the Pakistan Agriculture Research Council located at the National Research Centre Islamabad (NARC). Healthy seeds were selected through screening of seeds. The seeds were then sterilized with 3% sodium hypochlorite for 10 min. Seeds were then washed with distilled water three times; 20 healthy seeds were sown in each pot containing 800 mL of sand and 200 mL of water. Plants were grown in the laboratory by exposing them to 16 h of light and 8 h of dark and the temperature was kept at 20–25 °C under control conditions. Plants were irrigated daily with tap water. Fig schematic S1 explains the experimental workflow chart of this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: rGO NPs and Pb solutions preparation and application
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal Pb was applied as a stress to induce phytotoxicity in wheat while rGO NPs were used for its mitigation. Four treatments of rGO with concentrations of 15, 30, 60, and 120 mg/L were applied. While three different concentrations (300, 500, and 700 mg/L) of Pb were applied on 8th day after sowing. To check mitigative effects caused by rGO in stress condition combination of Pb 700 mg/L and rGO NPs 30 mg/L were also used on 8th day after sowing. Plants samples were collected by keeping a gap of one day (means 8th, 10th, 12th days old plants) for each treatment after the application of nanoparticles and Pb stress on 8th day after sowing.

The morphological parameters of wheat plants were measured for three independent replicates by repeating the same treatments in the same condition. Shoot length was measured in cm on the scale and their average values were calculated. The average root length was measured from the tip of the root to the base where shoots were attached to roots using a centimeter scale. With the help of an electronic scale, plant fresh weight was measured in mg. A chlorophyll meter (SPAD-502; Konica, Minolta sensing, Inc., Japan) was used for measuring The SPAD values for chlorophyll content in the leaves.

Stress-induced cell membrane damage was quantified with Evan's blue staining in accordance with the method used (Ullah et al., 2023). Fresh plant root parts were collected from control and differently treated plants and transferred into 2 mL Eppendorf tubes. After that, 2 mL of Evan's blue dye was added to each Eppendorf containing samples and was kept for 24 h. Then after 24 h of staining, they were properly washed with distilled water and the stained samples were then examined under light microscope.

125 mg of roots were ground with the help of an ice-cold pestle and mortar from each sample for each replicate. 1 mL of buffer was added to the extraction, the buffer consisted of 35 mL of 0.07 potassium phosphate buffer, 400 μL of 100 mM EDTA, 500 μL OF 200 mM ascorbic acid, and 2g Polyvinylpyrrolidone. The slurry was added to the Eppendorf tube and was centrifuged for 20 min at 15000 rpm after that the pellets formed were discarded and the supernatant was centrifuged for 20 min at 15000 rpm again. The supernatant was collected and used as an enzyme extract. The activity of CAT was measured by using already reported method. (Aebi, 1984) methodology. Through spectrophotometer, the optical density for CAT was calculated at 240 nm wavelength. For the calculation of APX activity (Nakano and Asada, 1981) methodology was used. At room temperature, APX concentration was measured at 290 nm wavelength for both blank and test solutions through a spectrophotometer. SOD contents investigation was conducted through (Verma and Dubey 2003) methodology. The blank and test solutions densities were measured at 560 nm wavelength for SOD activity with a spectrophotometer. The activity of POD was also estimated through (Velikova et al., 2000) methodology. The optical densities for the activity of POD for test and blank solutions were measured at 485 nm with the help of a spectrophotometer.
All the values were measured by applying the statistical analysis of data which was completed by using two-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) suitable for CRD and correlation coefficient.

The greenly synthesized reduced GO nanoparticle were well characterization on the evidences shown by different spectroscopic techniques and later these nanoparticles have shown effective impact on morphological, biological and physiological in wheat plant under simulated lead as illustrated in Fig. 1 .

In current study rGO was prepared through modified “Hummer's method and was greenly reduced to rGO using Tecoma stans plant extract as capping/reducing agent (Mahmoud et al., 2022). The rGO NPs were well characterized via several techniques such as UV-VIS spectroscopy, FTIR, EDX and SEM. Initially, UV–vis spectroscopy was used for confirmation of rGO Nanoparticles fabricated by a biological method. The biological method for fabrication of nanomaterials with the help of bio reducing agent is effective, economical, and ecofriendly as compared to chemical and physical process as these added a lot of toxicants, load of financial pressure and many other drawbacks (Akbar et al., 2018; Gong et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023; Zaheer et al., 2023). Different time point such as 1,2,3,6 and 12 h were set as mixer of precursors of graphene with plant extract as a reducing agent. The brown GO paste after thermal reaction with reducing agent at different time point and turned into black by confirming from the UV-VIS spectroscopy of rGO NPs was done at a wavelength of 150–600 nm in various intervals from 1hr to 12 h. Absorbance was initially shown at around 230 nm which become stable after 6–12 h as shown in Fig. 2 (a) indicating presence of proteins and secondary metabolites which are involved in reducing and capping mechanisms of nanoparticles. Infect, the reaction mixture in the start was not mature and shown very weak absorbance at 1–2 h treatment mixture at thermal treatment. But as the reaction proceeded, on 6–12 h the sharp and stable peak has shown clear peak. The plant extract which is main reducing agent has played key role for reduction of GO into GO NPs which confirmation from previous studies (Hasan et al., 2020). There are two characteristic features used as means of identification, the one maximum at 230 nm–265 nm which corresponds to π → π* transition of aromatic carbon molecular structures, and the shoulder occurs at approximately 300 nm corresponding to n → π* type transition between CO bonding (Farid et al., 2017; Hasan et al., 2018a). Further confirmation was also carried by FTIR spectra of greenly reduced GO are given in Fig. 2 (b), which includes several distinct bands in the range of 422–3705 cm−1 wavenumbers. All these bands were appeared due to certain different types of vibration, which specifically describe certain characteristics by the functional groups of greenly rGO such as the sharp broadband at 3179.37 cm−1 representing hydroxyl (OH) functional group of rGO in which the OH group is intermolecularly bonded, 1720.237 cm−1 represent CO stretching, at 1615.88 cm−1 amine (N–H) functional group is present, at 1228.14 cm−1 alkyl aryl ether (C–O) functional group is residing, at 1032.463 cm-1 sulfoxide (SO) functional group is present, while at 435.39 and 420.89 cm−1 monosubstituted C–H containing functional groups are found. The peaks in 1039.63, 1228.14, 1615.88, 1723.14, and 3171.89 cm−1 are found to decrease and disappear dramatically, which indicates the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups in GO this is an indication of the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups in GO (Varaprasad et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019). From the rGO spectra, it is confirmed that most of the oxygen-containing functional groups are removed from GO and hence its reduction occurred. But still, some residual functional group of oxygen exists on the surface of rGO. Above mention results showed enough evidences for the conformation of reduction of graphene oxide with the help of bio reducing agent which is ecofriendly and compatible. The current studies shown that presence of functional group by showing the evidences from the FTIR (Hasan et al., 2024; Zafar et al., 2024) result which confirmed the previous synthesis and characterizations of nanoparticles by biological methods and presence of biomolecules acts as reducing agent (Huang et al., 2023a; Luo et al., 2021; Zafar et al., 2021).

The SEM analysis showed that the greenly rGO NPs have polygonal spherical shapes with a particle size range from 9.67 to 11.57 nm (Fig. 2cd). The rGO NPs were not uniform shaped but still showing well morphology which indicates the conformation of rGO NPs synthesis however, there is less aggregation of NPs might be very effective physiological functional in application (Azizighannad and Mitra, 2018; Jiříčková et al., 2022; Priliana et al., 2022). The Morphological analysis of reduced graphene oxide indicates the wide range of application in the field of biomedicine as well as in agriculture and industrial wastes degradation and bioremediation as already reports shown effective results (Yazicilar et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023).

Zeta potential acts as a crucial indicator for surface potential charge and assigning stability to nanoparticles in aqueous suspensions. Biologically prepared rGO NPs exhibit a potential value of −20.6 mV, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), which symbolizes the presence of a negative charge on nanoparticles. Understanding Zeta potential is necessary to comprehend the stability of nanoparticles in aqueous suspensions (Ali et al., 2023; Javed et al., 2024; Mahmood et al., 2023a; Yu et al., 2023). Literature studies suggest that nanoparticles having zeta potential values higher than +30 mV and less than −30 mV are thought to be stable in the dispersion medium. The Zeta potential value of rGO highlights the long-term stability and compatibility (Dang et al., 2014; Hasan et al., 2014; Lv et al., 2016).

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) is very effective and influential technique that was expended to conduct elemental analysis of the biologically prepared rGO NPs. From Fig. 3c–e showed the presence of graphene and oxygen together and isolated form by red and green color confirmed the preparation of NPs. These results also matched with other workers preparing graphene and other nanomaterials by using different method as well (Huang et al., 2023a, 2023b; Saif et al., 2023). From EDS spectra presence of carbon and oxygen is confirmed with percentage values of 64.45 % for carbon and 35.55 for oxygen in greenly rGO NPs (Fig. 3b). Early reported data showed similar result and confirmed the current study validation regarding the elemental analysis of reduced graphene oxide nanoparticles with the help of biological method (Huang et al., 2023a, 2023b; Mahmood et al., 2023a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Pb on wheat morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: Morphological characteristics of wheat plants were investigated at three different concentrations of Pb stress. To investigate the effect of Pb on plant morphological parameters such as root, shoot, and body weight on 8th, 10th, and 12th days old plant of wheat was exposed to Pb stress of three different concentrations (300, 500, and 700 mg/L). Significant variations were noted in all three replicates of plant harvested with the gap of 2 days after treatment in comparison to control plants as shown in Fig. 3. Results demonstrate that Pb causes serious damage to the plant's shoot and root length and significant reduction was noted in all the lengths of plants. On days 10th and 12th significant reduction was noted for plants treated with 700 mg/L concentration of Pb in comparison to control plants and other treatments of Pb shown in Fig. 4 (a-c). The reduction in plant shoot and root length was observed at the 10th and 12th day Fig. 4(ab).

The fresh weight of the plants was investigated in response to the Pb stress. Three different concentrations (300, 500, 700 mg/L) of Pb were applied to wheat plants. Significant variations were observed for Pb stress in fresh-weight of plants for all the concentrations of Pb stresses as shown in Fig. 4 (c). Results demonstrate that in comparison to the control plants, and other treatments of Pb, 700 mg/L was found to significantly reduce the total fresh weight of plants. This means that 700 mg/L of Pb caused more damage to the plants and reduced their fresh weight for both 10th and 12th days old plants in comparison to Pb 300 and 500 mg/L. Further in our study to check mitigation in response to Pb stress through rGO, 700 mg/L of Pb stress was used. Current work nanomaterials influence on plant has shown similar effect reported before (Batool et al., 2022a, 2022b; Hamed et al., 2023; Hussain et al., 2023; Mahmood et al., 2023b; Qasim et al., 2020; Yousaf et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morpho-physiological characterization of wheat plant under rGO NPs application
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, morphological characteristics of 8 days old wheat plants were also investigated at four different concentrations (15, 30, 60, and 120 mg/L) of greenly rGO NPs for three days with one day of gap to optimize the condition of rGO NPs. The statistically analyzed data have shown significant variation among all treatments concerning time exposure in terms of all parameters Fig. 4 (d-e). The root length results show that rGO NPs caused significant promotion in root length. On days 10th and 12th after sowing and stress application on day 8th, all rGO NPs caused root elongation positively in comparison with the control except for rGO 15 mg/L which didn't carry the trend as the other did and have almost the same results as that for the control Fig. 4. (d). Over all, 30 mg/L of rGO caused a maximum increase in root length of wheat plants in comparison to control and other rGO treatments. According to the experimental findings, rGO NPs have a significant impact on the shoot length of wheat plants Fig. 4. (e). At day 10th maximum increase in average shoot length of 16.80 cm was noted for 60 mg/L of GO in comparison to that of control and other rGO NPs treatments of the same day plants in all the replicates. The maximum average plant fresh weight was observed at day 12th by 30 mg/L treated plants followed by 60 and 120 mg/L treatments of rGO NPs respectively for wheat plants. The shoot weight of wheat plants increases for all the rGO treatments in comparison with control, but a trend was observed where the maximum value for shoot length was noted for 30 mg/L (116.45 mg) of rGO followed by 120 mg/L (114.21 mg) of rGO. 3.6 Morphological characterization of wheat plant under Pb and rGO NPs stresses Fig. 4 (f).

To investigate the effective influence of Pb on wheat plant morphology and their mitigation through rGO nanoparticles, 700 mg/L of concentrations of Pb,30 mg/L concentration of rGO NPs, and a combination of Pb and rGO NPs (700 mg/L + 30 mg/L) were used Fig. 4 (g-i). The results demonstrate a significant variation in all parameters of plants at these concentrations over different time exposures. In a combination treatment of Pb and rGO, the average root length of all three replicates was found to be 23.28 cm which shows an increase in term of 19.81 cm for Pb where reduction is clear in the root length of plants Fig. 4 (g). The average shoot length for all three replicate Pb treatments of 700 mg/L at day 10th was found to be 16.14 cm, for the control it was 16.3 cm, and for rGO 30 mg/L was 17.25 cm approximately the same as the control. For day 12th the differences were clearer than that of day 10th as shoot length under Pb stress of 700 mg/L was found to reduce in comparison to the control and rGO treatment as shown in Fig. 4 (h). In combined treatment of Pb 700 mg/L and rGO 30 mg/L, it was observed that plants average fresh weight of all three replicates increases in comparison to the only Pb stress plants. On day 10th of the treatment plant's average fresh weight was found to be 210.59 mg for the Pb + rGO NPs combination and for only Pb-stressed plants it was 184.50 mg Fig. 4 (i).

In current study lead nitrate was applied to determine effects of Pb on wheat plants which is one of the most frequently occurring damaging toxic heavy metals to plants (Shehzad and Mustafa, 2023). Most of the Pb ions are absorbed by plants from the soil which remain concentrated in the roots, and its small portion transferred to the stems and leaves through translocation process. Pb ions exert toxic effects to the tissues of plants and damage the photosynthetic system of the plant (Sun et al., 2021). There is no any biological function of Pb, in contrary according to (Souahi et al., 2021) that it can only disrupt the physiological, morphological, and biochemical functions of wheat plants. In high concentrations, it was found to be key in reduction of seed germination in rice and also reduce other growth parameters of plants (Fahr et al., 2013; Pant et al., 2020). Current results were matching with the previous reported results on wheat plants as shown in Fig. 4a–c. Root cells are in direct contact with the Pb stress in the soil, so root is very sensitive to Pb which inhibits root growth and elongation (Garua and Sharma, 2021; Panthi and Park, 2022). Under Pb stress reduction in shoot length as observed in our study as well Fig. 4b may be due to disruption in micronutrient (Mg, K, Ca, and Na, etc.) absorbed by plants causing reduction in cell division in apical meristems of roots (Prusty et al., 2021). An increase in ROS level may be the other possible reason for it, as higher level of ROS causes damage to cellular metabolism which in turn can result in the reduction of plant growth (Alam et al. 2017).

In the current study it was observed that root length and biomass was reduced. It was revealed that transporter cells are present in roots which release different chelators that are implicated in the uptake of Pb in plants (Noor et al., 2022). From the study work of (Fatemi et al., 2020) (2020), it was observed that the root length of the B. juncea plant was found to be more adversely affected due to the uptake of Pb in higher levels by roots. In our study Fig. 4a decrease in root length of wheat plant was observed which may possibly be due to impairing water capacity and nutrient absorption caused by Pb stress.

A decrease in plant weight has been reported in various earlier research studies under Pb stress by (Lee et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). In the current research work (Fig. 4c), it was observed that Pb stress has significantly reduced the fresh plant weight of wheat plants. Pb interferes with photosynthesis by altering the photosynthetic pigment's synthetic process, blocking the electron transport chain reaction and stopping the dark reaction by inhibiting important enzymes of the Calvin cycle, and limiting CO2 availability by causing damage to stomatal cells (Fatemi et al., 2020). All these factors are responsible for stunted growth in plants and hence resulted in reduction of total plant weight.

Appropriate concentration of GO can be found to have positive effects on plants and the concentration may change from specie to species, the particle size of GO should be considered before application in agriculture (Wiśniewski, 2022). In the current study Fig. 4 (g-i) rGO was found to have positive impact on wheat plant growth as it is evident from the work of (Xu et al., 2015; Y. Yang et al., 2022) who demonstrated that GO have shown positive effects at 50 mg/L, and enhances the capacity of photosynthesis in leaves, which in turn improves plant yield and root/shoot morphological characters of plants and improves plant nutrients. In comparison to leaves effect of GO was found more obvious in roots than in leaves. In Festuca arundinacea a small concentration of GO such as 0.2 mg/L was found could have increased the height and biomass of the plant (Zhou et al., 2023). Our results are in accordance with the research work of (Guo et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023) where it was observed that treating tomatoes with 50 and 100 mg/L GO concentrations of GO could promote the plant's growth, in the same way in the current studies it was observed that growth of wheat plant increases at 30 and slightly at 60 mg/L of rGO. Current studies Fig. 4. (h) demonstrates that rGO enhance root growth, 15–120 mg/L of rGO show great influence on root growth in comparison to aerial parts (Wang et al., 2008; Y. Yang et al., 2022). In another reports, 30 and 60 mg/L concentration of GO was found to promote root growth in wheat seedlings and elongation was noted in the rhizome of rice for same concentrations. In the same way in comparison to control, 50 mg/L of GO was found to promote root length, volume, and total number of root tips and forks in maize seedlings (Y. Yang et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2023).

Growth promotion in tomato plants is due to the application of GO, which may stimulate cell division in the root and shoot parts of the plant (Guo et al., 2021). Zhang et al. (2019) revealed that GO promotes the growth of Aloe vera plants by stimulating the photosynthesis process. Similarly, Zhang et al. (2019) have found that in elm-cut seedlings 50 mg/L of GO treatment could promote the rate of growth by increasing the density of stomata in leaves, stomatal conductance, and the concentration of CO2 inside leaves, to improve the photosynthetic efficiency of plants (Y. Yang et al., 2022). Very few studies have been done in which the mitigative impact of graphene-based products has been investigated in green plants. However, in our study rGO was found to have positive effects on wheat plants in Pb stressed plants in contrast to other researchers Where in our studies 30 mg/L of rGO was found to mitigate and causes an increase in the activity of antioxidant enzyme (Guo et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2008) to overcome Pb stress. From all the above findings one thing is clear GO if used in an appropriate concentration promotes plant growth but the dose may vary from specie to specie in different plants. Our results are consistence with all the above-mentioned results.

The chlorophyll content of fresh leaves of wheat plants exposed to different concentrations of Pb 700 mg/L, rGO NPs 30, 60, and 120 mg/L, a combination of Pb and rGO NPs (700 mg/L and 30 mg/L respectively) was measured with the help of SPAD meter and the data was statistically analyzed. Results in Fig. 5 , show significant variation in chlorophyll content at different concentrations exposed at different time intervals for all the plants. SPAD chlorophyll level was significantly enhanced by rGO NPs in comparison with control of all the days, while plants exposed to Pb stress were observed to show a reduction in chlorophyll content in all day's plants of wheat. It was noted that application of rGO NPs decreased the toxicity caused by Pb in all day's plant and increased the SPAD chlorophyll content under Pb stress. Significant enhancement in the chlorophyll content (Fig. 5) of wheat plants to Pb + rGO NPs was present in all day's plants in comparison to control plants.

Chlorophyll content is an important indicator for stressful conditions in plants. Heavy metals show negative impact on plant chlorophyll during stress conditions (Mustafa and Komatsu, 2016; Noor et al., 2022). Changes in chlorophyll content in response to changes in environmental conditions deliberately have an impact on a plant's health. In our study, significant reduction was noted in leaf chlorophyll content when exposed to Pb stress (Priliana et al., 2022; Siddiqui et al., 2021) which may result due to the following factors: distortion in the ultrastructure of chloroplast, obstruction in electron transport system, enzyme inhibition of Calvin cycle, disruption in essential elements uptake, such as Fe and Mg. In our study Fig. (5), by applying different concentrations of rGO to wheat plants in accordance with (B. Yang et al., 2022; Y. Yang et al., 2022) chlorophyll content was found to increase for rGO treatment of 30 mg/L and in comparison, to the control and for rGO and Pb combination as well. In plants rGO enhances uptake and utilization of nutrients, nitrogen in plant leaves is in strong correlation with plant's photosynthetic capacity, and hence plant photosynthetic rate increases by increase in nitrogen content (Zhou et al., 2023). Cu and Fe are the required cofactors of the many of proteins which are involved in photo-synthetically electron transfer, which could possibly promote synthesis of chlorophyll and increase photosynthetic performance of plants by interacting with other important micronutrients synergistically (Nekoukhou et al., 2023). From the research work of Zhang et al. (2019) it is evident that higher concentrations of GO could decrease chlorophyll content which means that higher doses of GO have a negative impact on the chlorophyll content of plants which may alter species to species. As a result, we can conclude that GO is concentration dependent and must be applied in a specified dose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histochemical analysis and antioxidant enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: Damage resulting from Pb stress and amelioration caused by rGO NPs was observed in the roots of wheat plants stained with Evan's blue dye 2 and day 4 with different treatment as shown in Fig. 6 .(a-n). Significant variations were observed in the root anatomy at day 10th and 12th plants after treatment in comparison to the control. Damage was noted in the roots for Pb 700 as in Fig. 6(f,m) while rGO 30 and rGO 60 showed almost no damage to roots Fig. 6(c,d,j,k) but for rGO 120 damage was observed in roots cells Fig. 6(e,l). In comparison to Pb stress, the combined treatment of Pb and rGO showed less damage to root cells for both days shown in Fig. 6(g,n).

Measurement of different antioxidant enzymes of wheat plant produced in response to Pb stress of 700 mg/L, rGO NPs 15, 30, 60, 120 mg/L, and Pb and rGO NPs and combination (700 mg/L+ 30 mg/L) stresses was done Fig. 7 .(a-d). Reduction was noted in Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity in the roots of Pb-treated plants in comparison to control for all days in wheat plants. NPs of rGO alone and in combination with Pb stress showed a significant enhancement in the activity of SOD in root in comparison to the plants only just treated with Pb stress Fig. 7(a). The activity of Peroxidase (POD) was enhanced in the Pb-treated root of wheat plants, but POD activity was maximally enhanced by rGO treatments alone or in combination with Pb stress in comparison to that of control as evident from Fig. 7(b).

An antioxidant enzyme Catalase is produced by plants in response to counteract the toxicity caused by stress. The CAT activity was investigated in wheat plants in response to the stress produced by Pb and rGO NPs treatments. A substantial decrease in CAT activity was found for Pb treatment on day 10th after sowing and treated at day 8th in roots of wheat plants in comparison to control which was found to increase on day 12th, while a significant increase was found in the activity of CAT in response to different concentrations of rGO NPs in roots of wheat plants of all days in comparison with control except rGO 120 mg/L where CAT activity was found almost same on both days 10th and 12th. rGO NPs (30 mg/L) in combination with Pb (700 mg/L) stress condition have significantly enhanced the CAT activity in roots of wheat plants in comparison with the control and Pb treated plants, which illustrate the mitigative function of the rGO NPs against the damaging effects produced by the Pb in stress condition Fig. 7(c). However, another important enzyme Ascorbate peroxidase (APX) was also found to be an increase in the activity of Pb stressed plants treated with rGO NPs which shows that rGO NPs can reduce the oxidative stress of Pb by stopping their damaging effects to plants. The APX activity was found to be more on day 12th while treating plants with Pb + rGO NPs on day 8th of sowing in comparison to control and other treatments as shown in Fig. 7(d).

Heavy metal stress results in excess production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which in plants may either directly or indirectly cause cellular damage (Elsayed et al., 2022). Heavy metals reported by (Chibuike and Obiora, 2014; Zunaidi et al., 2021) take part in Fenton and Haber–Weiss reactions, where OH• radicals are being generated from the decomposition of H2O2. Macromolecules such as carbohydrates, lipids, proteins and nucleic acid are being damaged by the ROS produced which subsequently may results in aging and plant death (Francolino et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2022). Anti-oxidative defense systems comprising CAT, APX, SOD, POD, and many other enzymatic and non-enzymatic defenses always play an important role, while safeguarding plants from adverse oxidative injuries during stress conditions (Hutton et al., 2016). Nanoparticles (NPs) were found to enhance antioxidant enzyme activities described in earlier research works (Atarod et al., 2016). In the current study, significant reduction and enhancement in the antioxidant enzyme activities under rGO NPs was observed in wheat plants. Our results are in accordance with the research work of (Khan et al., 2021) who demonstrated the changes in antioxidant enzyme activities in wheat plants exposed to rGO NPs treatments, while reduction in wheat growth and biomass due to induction in oxidative stress caused by Pb stress was observed in wheat plant (Yazicilar et al., 2022).

CAT is one of the important antioxidant enzymes that scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) to break excess H2O2 into water and oxygen to protect the cells from oxidative and corrosive effects of heavy metals stress. In our study by increasing Pb stress decrease in CAT activity occurred and has shown the similar result as previously presented by (Munir et al., 2022; Yazicilar et al., 2022) where by increasing Pb stress CAT activity decreases in the roots of wheat plants. According to the work of (Guo et al., 2021) it has been observed that CAT activity increases by increasing GO concentration in plant roots. (Gheshlaghpour et al., 2021) observed that APX activity was enhanced by Pb stress in plants. In the current work, APX activity was found enhanced by GO alone and in combination with Pb in comparison to control treatment in wheat plants to scavenge H2O2 production. The possible reason for enhancement in APX activity might be related to the higher level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced due to Pb stress. This increase in APX activity can combat H2O2 produced in cells, to decrease the cellular membrane damage caused by oxidative stress. The activity of SOD was found to elevate in response to GO treatment in the root of wheat plants as was observed in earlier research by Gao et al. (2019). In a similar way in the research work of (Chang et al., 2012; Y. Yang et al., 2022) increase in SOD activity was observed for wheat plants treated with GO. An increase in SOD activity was observed for Pb stress in the roots of wheat plants in comparison to control by following the results of Navabpour et al. (2020). Further increase was noted for SOD activity in combined treatment of rGO and Pb stress. Our results are in accordance with both (Abdelkhalek and Al-Askar, 2020; Hasan et al., 2018b; Zulfiqar et al., 2019) for Pb stress in wheat plants. GO was found to increase POD activity where an increase in POD activity was discerned in plant roots as observed in our work. In current study it was observed that Pb in combination with rGO induces the activity of POD.

In conclusion, current study emphasizes the promising application of biologically synthesized rGO NPs in mitigating the toxic effects of Pb on wheat plants. This study found that rGO NPs stimulated plant growth and development via plant morphology, physiology, biochemical improvement, and high biomass accumulation in Pb stress. Furthermore, we have also identified an optimal concentration of 30 mg/L of rGO NPs as effective in reducing Pb toxicity in wheat plants. Additionally, we have observed significant improvements in chlorophyll levels and antioxidant activity, along with a reduction in Pb absorption when rGO is used in conjunction with Pb in wheat plants. The progressive impacts of green rGO NPs were well demonstrated in the form of photosynthetic pigments like chlorophyll, antioxidative parameters, and root anatomy. Our research outcome addresses the environmental stress issues and provide evidence to mitigate the outer stress via green nanotechnology for wide future application in agriculture to increase the productivity rate.
